patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 13-year-old 8th-grade club soccer player. Start by saying: “My knee hurts right under the kneecap, especially when I run or jump.” Volunteer that it came on gradually over the last few weeks during sports season, worsens with running/jumping/stairs, and is sore/tender when you press just below the kneecap. If asked about triggers, add it hurts more with squats, kneeling, and lunges; rest helps but it returns with practice; it can feel stiff at the start then loosens; there’s a small bump/puffy sensitive spot at the bottom of the kneecap; no single injury you can recall. Only if asked about training/changes, reveal you’ve increased practice and jumping, have fewer rest days, feel tight hamstrings/thighs, changed shoes/trained on a harder surface, avoid fully bending the knee, and it’s worse after sitting then standing. Do NOT volunteer that you take extra pain pills before games; admit it only if directly asked about meds. Express worry it might be a growth/bone or inherited joint problem. Bring up cost concerns and preference for a “quick fix” and online exercises. If the clinician shows empathy, respond appreciatively and become more open.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease
required_evidence: Anterior knee pain localized to the inferior pole of the patella, worse with running/jumping/squatting and improved with rest
Tenderness at inferior patellar pole or proximal patellar tendon insertion; pain reproduced with resisted knee extension or patellar tendon loading
Typical age/activity context (adolescent with recent growth spurt and/or repetitive sports activity)
No systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) and no inflammatory pattern

Imaging (if obtained): X-ray may show fragmentation/irregularity or calcification at inferior patellar pole; ultrasound/MRI may show proximal patellar tendinopathy/apophysitis without intra-articular pathology
Labs (if obtained) not suggestive of infection/inflammatory arthritis

medications: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 40 mg/kg/day; not to exceed adult max)

non_pharmacological: Relative rest and activity modification: avoid/limit jumping, sprinting, deep squats/kneeling until pain controlled
Ice to inferior patellar pole 15-20 minutes after activity, 3-4 times/day during flare
Physical therapy/home program: quadriceps and hamstring stretching; progressive eccentric quadriceps strengthening; hip/glute strengthening; gradual return-to-sport plan
Patellar tendon strap or supportive taping during activity if helpful
Footwear review and training load management; avoid sudden increases in intensity/volume
Consider imaging (knee X-ray) if atypical presentation, significant swelling/effusion, inability to bear weight, trauma, or symptoms persist despite conservative care
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks (earlier in 1-2 weeks if worsening pain, new swelling/effusion, night pain, or functional decline); return-to-sport when pain-free with sport-specific drills and no tenderness

must_include: This is an overuse irritation at the lower end of the kneecap where the patellar tendon attaches (apophysitis), common during growth spurts
It is not an infection and not usually a problem inside the knee joint
Symptoms often improve with time and load management; condition is self-limited
Relative rest/activity modification is the main treatment; pain-guided return to sport
Use ice and short-term pain control (NSAID or acetaminophen) as needed
Stretching and strengthening (often via PT) to reduce tendon/apophysis stress
Expected course: improvement over weeks; full resolution may take several weeks to months depending on activity and adherence
Red flags requiring prompt reassessment: fever, significant swelling/effusion, inability to bear weight, night/rest pain, rapidly worsening symptoms, or new trauma
When imaging or specialist referral may be needed (persistent symptoms despite 6-12 weeks of conservative care, atypical exam, concern for alternative diagnosis)
Training load management: avoid sudden increases; incorporate rest days and cross-training
Warm-up/cool-down, flexibility work (quads/hamstrings), and gradual progression of jumping/running
Supportive measures (patellar strap, proper shoes) and adherence to PT/home exercises

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 4-year-old boy’s parent and a daycare assistant on leave. You speak clearly but are visibly anxious and worried.

Start by volunteering: your child “keeps getting stiff,” moves like an older person especially in the morning, is not outgrowing clothes/shorter than peers, face looks puffier/coarser, and he tires easily and can’t keep up playing.

If the clinician asks, share: frequent ear infections and possible hearing issues, loud snoring with brief pauses in breathing, big/protruding belly, past or suspected hernias, increased clumsiness/tripping, late crowded teeth and thick gums.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: limited ability to raise arms or straighten fingers, shortness of breath on stairs, chronic constipation, worse vision in dim light, long-standing hoarse voice.

Resist/avoid unless trust is built: bedwetting (embarrassing), teasing at school, missed follow-ups and guilt.

Offer misconceptions: wonder about juvenile arthritis, hypothyroidism, or allergies.

Express financial fears about insurance denying genetic tests/specialists, job risk, and misunderstanding palliative care. If met with empathy, soften, admit overwhelm, and ask for help navigating coverage and referrals.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mucolipidosis (most consistent with mucolipidosis type II/III spectrum)
required_evidence: Progressive multisystem involvement consistent with lysosomal storage disease (e.g., coarse facial features, skeletal dysplasia/contractures, short stature, developmental delay, recurrent respiratory infections, hepatosplenomegaly, cardiac valve disease)
History/physical suggesting ML II/III (gingival hypertrophy, stiff joints, kyphosis/scoliosis, limited range of motion)
Supportive laboratory pattern may include elevated alkaline phosphatase from bone involvement with otherwise non-specific CBC/CMP
Definitive confirmation requires specialized testing (not in provided labs): elevated lysosomal enzyme activities in plasma with low activity in fibroblasts; GNPTAB/GNPTG gene testing

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal to mildly low (mild anemia possible in chronic disease)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Typically normal (normocytic) unless concurrent nutritional deficiency
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; may be mildly elevated during intercurrent infection
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Usually normal to mildly elevated (non-specific; may rise with intercurrent illness)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - ALT: Usually normal to mildly elevated (non-specific; may rise with intercurrent illness)

non_pharmacological: Refer to genetics/metabolic specialist for confirmatory testing (GNPTAB/GNPTG) and counseling
Multidisciplinary supportive care: physical therapy/occupational therapy for contractures and mobility; speech/swallow evaluation if feeding issues; audiology and ophthalmology screening
Cardiology evaluation (echo) for valvular disease/cardiomyopathy; pulmonology assessment for restrictive disease/OSA and airway management planning

Discuss prognosis and goals of care; connect family to community resources and support groups

must_include: Mucolipidosis is a genetic lysosomal storage disorder affecting multiple organs (bones/joints, heart, lungs, growth and development)

Different subtypes (II vs III) vary in severity and expected course

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mercury poisoning (suspected chronic inorganic/elemental exposure)
acceptable_alternatives: Mercury toxicity (unspecified form) pending speciation and exposure history
Heavy metal toxicity (mercury most likely) pending confirmation
required_evidence: Elevated mercury level above reference range on blood and/or urine testing (urine corrected to creatinine preferred for chronic exposure)

Exposure history consistent with mercury (e.g., artisanal gold mining, thermometer/fluorescent bulb breakage in enclosed space, industrial exposure, skin-lightening creams, contaminated supplements/folk remedies, frequent high-mercury fish if methylmercury suspected)

Compatible clinical syndrome (neuropsychiatric symptoms such as tremor/irritability/insomnia/cognitive changes; peripheral neuropathy; gingivostomatitis; GI symptoms; renal findings such as proteinuria)
Assessment for end-organ involvement, especially renal dysfunction (creatinine/eGFR, proteinuria)

expected_lab_results.Blood mercury (total): Elevated above reference range; may be more prominent with recent exposure and with methylmercury from seafood (total blood mercury elevated).
expected_lab_results.Urine mercury (spot, corrected to creati: Elevated above reference range; typically more sensitive for chronic elemental/inorganic mercury exposure.
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: May be normal or elevated depending on renal involvement; monitor for rise suggesting nephrotoxicity.
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be normal or elevated consistent with renal impairment if present.
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): May be normal or reduced if mercury-associated nephrotoxicity is present.
expected_lab_results.Urine protein/creatinine ratio: May be elevated if clinically significant proteinuria is present.
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - RBC: May be normal; can show microscopic hematuria in some renal injury patterns.

medications: Dimercaprol (BAL) 3 mg/kg IM every 4 hours for 2 days, then every 6 hours for 1 day, then every 12 hours for 10 days (for severe inorganic mercury toxicity or significant symptoms; specialist-guided)

Succimer (DMSA) 10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours for 5 days, then 10 mg/kg PO every 12 hours for 14 days (for mild-to-moderate symptomatic inorganic/elemental mercury toxicity; consider if renal function allows and per toxicology guidance)
non_pharmacological: Immediate removal from suspected mercury source; arrange environmental/occupational assessment and decontamination guidance
Consult medical toxicology/poison control for chelation selection, dosing, and monitoring
Supportive care: hydration as appropriate; manage nausea, tremor, neuropathic pain symptomatically
Avoid high-mercury fish and any suspected contaminated supplements/creams until clarified
Assess for and manage renal involvement (quantify proteinuria; nephrology referral if significant proteinuria or reduced eGFR)
Repeat mercury levels (blood/urine) and renal studies to document decline after exposure cessation/chelation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck symptoms and renal labs within 48–72 hours if chelation started or if symptomatic; otherwise within 1–2 weeks with repeat urine mercury (Cr-corrected) and renal panel, then serially every 2–4 weeks until improving/stable.

must_include: Mercury exposure can cause neurologic and kidney problems; diagnosis is supported by elevated blood/urine mercury plus exposure history
Blood vs urine mercury reflect different exposure types/timing; urine (Cr-corrected) is often used for chronic elemental/inorganic exposure
Need to identify and stop the source to prevent ongoing toxicity
Primary treatment is removing exposure; chelation is considered when levels are elevated with symptoms and/or significant exposure
Chelation medicines (succimer or dimercaprol) bind mercury to help eliminate it; they require monitoring and may have side effects
Plan for repeat mercury levels and kidney monitoring to ensure improvement
Review red flags requiring urgent care: worsening confusion, severe tremor/weakness, shortness of breath after inhalation exposure, decreased urine output, swelling, or severe abdominal pain
Discuss potential need for specialist involvement (toxicology, nephrology) and possible workplace/public health reporting depending on source
Confirm medication allergies (penicillin causes hives) and clarify no known chelator allergies
Avoid suspected sources (broken devices, industrial exposure without protection, skin creams/supplements of unknown origin)
If seafood-related exposure is possible, avoid high-mercury fish (e.g., shark, swordfish, king mackerel, tilefish) until advised
Hand hygiene and safe handling/cleanup guidance; do not vacuum visible mercury; follow local hazardous materials recommendations

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit exposure history (elemental/inorganic/organic), timing, route (inhalation/ingestion/dermal), symptom profile (neuro/renal/GI/respiratory), occupational/home sources, and pregnancy/lactation status; screen for red flags requiring urgent care.

Order appropriate mercury testing and baseline organ assessment: urine mercury (creatinine-corrected) and/or blood mercury depending on exposure type/timing; CMP/creatinine/BUN, urinalysis (protein/hematuria), CBC; consider additional tests based on scenario (e.g., CXR/pulse ox for inhalation exposure).
Interpret mercury levels in clinical context (exposure type and timing) and assess renal/hepatic function to guide need for chelation and monitoring.
State suspected/confirmed mercury poisoning based on exposure history plus elevated blood/urine mercury; document differential and severity assessment.

Explain diagnosis and implications; emphasize source identification and removal; provide safe cleanup/avoidance guidance and fish/skin cream/supplement counseling; review red flags and when to seek urgent care; discuss possible specialist/public health involvement.

Critical safety check before prescribing chelation: confirm medication allergies (including penicillin causing hives) and specifically ask about prior reactions to chelators (succimer/dimercaprol) and excipients.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interaction risks and avoid concurrent metal supplements that may interfere with chelation plan.

If indicated by symptoms and elevated levels/significant exposure, initiate appropriate chelation (e.g., succimer for many non-severe cases; dimercaprol for severe inorganic mercury toxicity), with monitoring plan and clear return precautions; if not indicated, document rationale and focus on exposure cessation and monitoring.
Arrange repeat mercury levels and kidney monitoring, reassess symptoms, and coordinate toxicology/nephrology referral if needed; ensure exposure remediation steps are completed.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep are present
Daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, impaired concentration, mood disturbance, reduced performance)
Frequency at least 3 nights per week
Duration: at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or <3 months for short-term insomnia)

Rule out primary contributors: substance/medication effects (including caffeine, alcohol, stimulants), poor sleep hygiene, circadian rhythm disorder, untreated pain, depression/anxiety, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms
Thyroid dysfunction, anemia/iron deficiency, and B12 deficiency considered/excluded based on history and labs

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range; no evidence of diabetes contributing to nocturia or sleep disruption
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Normal; no anemia contributing to fatigue/sleep complaints
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal (or low-normal); if low, consider iron deficiency and assess for restless legs symptoms

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (optional; especially if sleep-onset difficulty)
Doxepin 3-6 mg PO nightly (for sleep-maintenance insomnia; use lowest effective dose)
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid screens/bright light before bed, reserve bed for sleep/sex only
Limit caffeine (avoid after noon) and nicotine; avoid alcohol as a sleep aid
Wind-down routine/relaxation training (e.g., progressive muscle relaxation, mindfulness)
Assess and address comorbid contributors (pain, anxiety/depression, GERD, nocturia); screen for OSA and restless legs based on symptoms
Review current supplement use (Choline) and timing; avoid taking stimulating supplements late in the day
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess sleep diary outcomes, daytime function, adverse effects, and need for medication adjustment; earlier if worsening mood, safety concerns, or suspected sleep apnea

must_include: Insomnia is defined by sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment despite adequate opportunity to sleep
Confirm pattern (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), frequency, and duration
Discuss common contributing factors and the plan to rule out/treat them (e.g., caffeine/alcohol, mood symptoms, pain, OSA, restless legs)
CBT-I is first-line and typically more durable than medications

If medication is used, choose lowest effective dose and shortest necessary duration; review expected benefits and side effects (next-day sedation, dizziness, anticholinergic effects for doxepin at higher doses)
Avoid combining sedatives or using alcohol for sleep
Set realistic expectations: improvement may take weeks; track with a sleep diary
Safety counseling: avoid driving/operating machinery if drowsy, especially after starting or changing sleep meds
When to seek urgent help: suicidal thoughts, severe mood changes, breathing pauses/snoring with choking, or concerning medication reactions
Consistent wake time and limiting time in bed to consolidate sleep
Caffeine/alcohol timing guidance
Screen/blue-light reduction and relaxation routine before bed
Exercise regularly but avoid vigorous exercise close to bedtime

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia history (onset/maintenance/early awakening), frequency/duration, daytime impairment, sleep opportunity/environment, and precipitating/maintaining factors.

Screen for contributors/comorbidities: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol/cannabis, other sedatives, mood/anxiety symptoms, pain, nocturia, GERD, shift work/jet lag, pregnancy status if relevant, OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, choking), restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, mania/hypomania.

Medication reconciliation and safety screen: current prescriptions/OTC/herbals (antihistamines, decongestants, stimulants), prior sleep meds tried, substance use, driving/operating machinery needs, fall risk.
Establish insomnia diagnosis when sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment occurs despite adequate opportunity to sleep; characterize type and chronicity; identify likely contributing factors.
Explain diagnosis and evaluation plan; introduce CBT-I as first-line; provide sleep hygiene and stimulus control/sleep restriction principles; set expectations and sleep diary tracking.

Optional targeted testing only if indicated by history (e.g., ferritin/iron studies if restless legs suspected; TSH if thyroid symptoms; pregnancy test if relevant). Routine labs are not required for uncomplicated insomnia.
Review any ordered test results to refine differential (e.g., iron deficiency contributing to RLS) before finalizing treatment.
Critical safety check before prescribing any sleep medication; confirm drug and class allergies and prior adverse reactions.
Critical safety check before prescribing; assess interactions with current meds/substances (e.g., CNS depressants, opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, CYP inhibitors/inducers).

Confirm contraindication-relevant history (e.g., severe COPD/OSA not evaluated, hepatic impairment, history of complex sleep behaviors with Z-drugs, substance use disorder, pregnancy/breastfeeding, glaucoma/urinary retention if considering anticholinergic agents, suicidality if considering sedating antidepressants).

If medication is appropriate after safety checks and shared decision-making, prescribe lowest effective dose for shortest duration; avoid poly-sedative regimens; counsel on side effects and next-day impairment.

Explain treatment plan details: CBT-I components, medication risks/benefits if used, avoidance of alcohol/sedative combinations, drowsy-driving precautions, and when to seek urgent care (suicidality, severe mood changes, OSA red flags, concerning reactions).
Arrange reassessment (typically 2–4 weeks) to review sleep diary, response to CBT-I, side effects, and taper/stop any short-term medication; escalate evaluation if persistent or red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and follow-up.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary angioedema (HAE) due to C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency/dysfunction (Type I or II)
acceptable_alternatives: Acquired angioedema due to C1-INH deficiency
required_evidence: Recurrent angioedema without urticaria (often facial, extremities, abdominal pain, or laryngeal symptoms)
Low C4 complement during attacks and typically between attacks
C1-INH functional activity low (Type I/II); antigenic C1-INH low in Type I and normal/high in Type II
Serum tryptase not elevated (helps exclude mast-cell mediated process)
C1q normal (supports hereditary rather than acquired C1-INH deficiency)

expected_lab_results.C4 complement: Low (typically below reference range), especially during attacks; often persistently low between attacks
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) antigenic: Type I: low; Type II: normal or elevated
expected_lab_results.C1q complement: Normal (would be low in acquired C1-INH deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - WBC: Usually normal (may be mildly elevated with stress/inflammation)

medications: C1 esterase inhibitor (human, plasma-derived) 20 units/kg IV once at onset of attack
OR Icatibant 30 mg SC once at onset of attack; may repeat every 6 hours if needed (max 3 doses/24 hours)
OR Ecallantide 30 mg SC once (where available; administer in setting capable of managing anaphylaxis)
If laryngeal involvement or airway symptoms: Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1 mg/mL) every 5-15 minutes as needed while securing airway (adjunct; does not treat bradykinin-mediated edema)
If frequent attacks (prophylaxis): Lanadelumab 300 mg SC every 2 weeks (may extend to every 4 weeks if well controlled)
Alternative prophylaxis: Berotralstat 150 mg PO daily with food
Short-term prophylaxis before procedures (e.g., dental/surgery): C1 esterase inhibitor (human) 20 units/kg IV 1-6 hours pre-procedure
non_pharmacological: Assess airway immediately; prepare for early intubation if voice change, stridor, tongue swelling, or progressive throat tightness
Avoid known triggers (trauma, stress, estrogen-containing contraceptives, ACE inhibitors); review medication list for bradykinin-related triggers
Provide an HAE action plan and ensure access to on-demand therapy for home use when appropriate
Refer to allergy/immunology for confirmation, family screening, and long-term management
Educate that antihistamines and corticosteroids are generally ineffective for HAE attacks; Alimemazine may help sedation/itch from other causes but does not treat HAE swelling
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks with allergy/immunology (earlier if any airway symptoms, recurrent attacks, or inadequate response to on-demand therapy)

must_include: HAE is bradykinin-mediated angioedema and typically occurs without hives
Low C4 and low C1-INH function support the diagnosis; normal tryptase argues against allergic/anaphylactic swelling

Start by saying: “He’s been having more episodes where he suddenly blanks out and drops things—I’m worried his seizures are getting worse.” Volunteer: frequent brief staring spells several times/day; sudden quick jerks causing him to drop objects; he’s nonverbal; more unsteady and needs help walking; weight gain.

Cytopenias may be present (e.g., anemia and/or leukopenia) with chronic inflammation

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for PRAAS/CANDLE: onset (often childhood), recurrent fevers, violaceous pernio/chilblain-like lesions, panniculitis/lipodystrophy, arthralgias/myalgias, fatigue, growth issues, triggers (cold), prior treatments and steroid dependence, infection history, family history/consanguinity, and review of systems for organ involvement (lungs, kidneys, eyes, GI).

Order baseline and diagnostic-support labs: CBC with differential, CMP (AST/ALT/Cr), ESR/CRP, ferritin, lipid panel, urinalysis/protein, CK, hepatitis B/C and TB screening prior to JAK inhibitor, pregnancy test if applicable, and consider interferon signature/genetic testing (PSMB8/related) if available.
Interpret labs for persistently elevated inflammatory markers, cytopenias, liver abnormalities, renal involvement, and infection screening results to support diagnosis and ensure treatment safety.

Make working diagnosis of proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome (PRAAS/CANDLE) based on symptom pattern plus inflammatory markers, and document differentials and why less likely (e.g., AOSD, SLE/vasculitis, chronic infection, malignancy).
Critical safety check before prescribing: verify allergies/intolerances to proposed meds (e.g., baricitinib, TMP-SMX if prophylaxis considered, NSAIDs, steroids) and past reactions.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., other immunosuppressants, strong OAT3 inhibitors affecting baricitinib, anticoagulants/antiplatelets in context of thrombosis risk).

Initiate disease-modifying therapy consistent with PRAAS: JAK inhibitor (e.g., baricitinib) with appropriate dosing considerations (renal function) and monitoring; consider short-term steroid burst/bridge for flare with explicit taper plan; ensure contraindications are addressed (active serious infection, severe cytopenias, severe hepatic impairment, pregnancy considerations).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, rationale and differential; explain treatment goals and steroid-sparing strategy with JAK inhibitor; address infection risk, warning signs, lab monitoring, medication risks (cytopenias, LFTs, thrombosis); provide lifestyle advice (cold avoidance/skin protection, adherence, no abrupt steroid stop, vaccines/infection prevention).

Arrange close follow-up for monitoring and shared decision-making: early reassessment (e.g., 2–4 weeks after starting JAK inhibitor) with repeat CBC/CMP/ESR/CRP and symptom review; coordinate rheumatology/dermatology/immunology as needed.
Close encounter after plan, safety netting, and follow-up are established.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You speak clearly but are anxious and worried about the future. Start by saying: “My ankles keep giving out and I keep tripping over my own feet.” Volunteer that your feet feel weak like you can’t lift them well, it’s been going on for years but worsening, you wear out the front/outside of shoes, and you have high arches with slightly curled toes.

If asked, add: thinning of lower legs/feet, pins-and-needles or numb toes at night, trouble running and faster fatigue on stairs/hills, occasional hand clumsiness (buttons/jars), repeated ankle sprains, worse balance in the dark/uneven ground, and a family pattern of “weak ankles/high arches/braces.”

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: reduced sensation to cuts/hot water, calluses/pressure spots, toe cramping/rubbing, avoiding long walks/social plans, and others noticing foot slapping.

Be reluctant about falls and how often you trip; you’re embarrassed and worried about job/driving and insurance implications. Mention misconceptions: pinched back nerve, diabetes, or just being out of shape. If the clinician is empathic, you open up more and admit feeling down/anxious.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (feet/legs > hands)
Distal sensory loss (reduced vibration/proprioception) and reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (pes cavus/high arches, hammer toes) and/or frequent ankle sprains
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (often autosomal dominant) OR onset in childhood/adolescence with long course
No acute systemic illness to explain neuropathy; pattern is length-dependent and symmetric
Electrodiagnostic testing planned/obtained (nerve conduction studies +/- EMG) to classify demyelinating vs axonal; genetic testing considered

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from CMT itself)

medications: Gabapentin 100-300 mg PO at bedtime, then titrate to 300 mg PO three times daily as tolerated for neuropathic pain
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for musculoskeletal pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neurology for confirmation/classification (NCS/EMG) and consideration of genetic testing
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, and fall-prevention strategies
Occupational therapy for hand weakness/adaptive devices if needed
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop; custom orthotics; supportive footwear
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformities (e.g., pes cavus/hammer toes) and surgical evaluation if severe
Home safety assessment and assistive devices as indicated (cane/walker) to reduce falls
Genetic counseling for patient/family planning and screening of at-risk relatives
Avoid or minimize exposure to medications known to worsen neuropathy where possible (review medication list)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, and medication tolerability in 4-6 weeks; sooner (1-2 weeks) if medication side effects or rapid functional decline

must_include: CMT is an inherited peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves
Typical course is slowly progressive; symptoms often start in feet/legs and can involve hands later
Diagnosis is supported by exam and confirmed/classified with nerve conduction studies and sometimes genetic testing
This is not contagious and is not caused by lifestyle; focus is on function and symptom control
No curative therapy currently; treatment aims to maintain mobility, prevent complications, and control pain
Role of PT/OT, braces (AFOs), and footwear/orthotics
How to take prescribed pain medication(s), expected benefits, and common side effects (e.g., gabapentin sedation/dizziness)
When to seek urgent care (new rapid weakness, new bowel/bladder symptoms, severe falls, concerning medication reactions)
Discuss inheritance patterns and offer genetic counseling/testing options
Discuss prognosis variability and realistic goals (maintain independence, reduce falls/pain)
Invite questions about work/school accommodations and driving/foot control if foot drop is present
Foot care and skin checks to prevent ulcers/injuries, especially with sensory loss
Fall-prevention strategies and safe exercise (low-impact aerobic activity, stretching)
Avoid alcohol excess and review any supplements/medications for neuropathy risk
Importance of follow-up and reporting progression or new neurologic symptoms

Order basic neuropathy mimics/complication screening as appropriate (e.g., A1c/glucose, B12, TSH, CMP) and document rationale to avoid misattributing treatable causes.
Review ordered lab results and integrate into differential; ensure no alternative etiology requiring different management.

Make/confirm working diagnosis of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease based on history/exam context; recommend confirmatory classification with nerve conduction studies ± genetic testing; assess severity and complications (foot deformities, weakness, sensory loss).
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication (including neuropathic pain agents) to prevent allergic reactions.
Non-critical but expected safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., additive sedation with gabapentin + alcohol/benzodiazepines/opioids).

If symptomatic neuropathic pain is present, prescribe an appropriate first-line neuropathic pain option (e.g., gabapentin) with safe dosing considerations and monitoring; avoid contraindicated agents and set expectations.

Meet all communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (PT/OT, AFOs/orthotics, pain med instructions/side effects), address inheritance/prognosis/accommodations, and lifestyle/foot care/fall prevention/avoid neurotoxins; provide urgent return precautions.
Arrange follow-up to reassess function/pain/med tolerability, review results, and coordinate referrals (neurology, PT/OT, orthotics, genetic counseling) and confirmatory testing (NCS/EMG ± genetics).
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and a clear plan.

The agent explicitly screens for red flags inconsistent with typical CMT (rapid progression over days-weeks, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, acute asymmetric weakness, severe back pain with neurologic deficits) and escalates urgency if present.
The agent asks about family history of similar symptoms and frames CMT as inherited, while acknowledging de novo mutations and variable penetrance/expressivity.
The agent avoids claiming CMT is caused by lifestyle or contagion and clearly states it is not contagious and not due to personal fault.

The agent explains that diagnosis is supported by exam and confirmed/classified with nerve conduction studies and sometimes genetic testing; does not overpromise that genetics will always identify a mutation.

The agent distinguishes CMT from treatable mimics and considers basic screening for common neuropathy causes when clinically appropriate (e.g., diabetes, B12 deficiency, thyroid disease), especially if presentation is atypical or late-onset.

The agent provides an evidence-aligned management plan emphasizing function: PT/OT, stretching/strengthening within tolerance, braces (AFOs) for foot drop, orthotics/footwear, and fall-prevention strategies.

If prescribing for neuropathic pain, the agent uses appropriate counseling: how to take the medication, expected onset/benefit, common side effects (e.g., gabapentin sedation/dizziness), cautions about driving/operating machinery, and tapering guidance if stopping.
Before prescribing, the agent performs the required critical safety checks: allergy_check and contraindication_check; failure to do so is a critical error.
The agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when feasible; missing it is non-critical but should reduce score.
The agent avoids neurotoxic or inappropriate medications and advises reviewing supplements/medications for neuropathy risk; counsels to avoid alcohol excess due to neuropathy and fall risk.
The agent addresses inheritance patterns and offers genetic counseling/testing options, including implications for family planning and testing of relatives when appropriate.

The agent discusses prognosis as slowly progressive with variability, sets realistic goals (maintain independence, reduce falls/pain), and explores work/school accommodations and driving/foot control if foot drop is present.
The agent includes foot care education: daily skin checks, proper footwear, prompt treatment of sores, and attention to reduced sensation to prevent ulcers/injuries.

The agent provides clear return precautions: new rapid weakness, new bowel/bladder symptoms, severe or increasing falls, and concerning medication reactions (e.g., severe rash, swelling, breathing difficulty, suicidal ideation, profound sedation).
The agent uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding (teach-back), invites questions, and documents shared decision-making.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
acceptable_alternatives: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) with mixed ketoacidosis
Starvation ketoacidosis (if diabetes excluded and history supports)
required_evidence: High anion gap metabolic acidosis suggested by low bicarbonate (CO2) with clinical dehydration/tachypnea
Electrolyte derangements consistent with DKA (often low sodium due to hyperglycemia-related dilution; potassium may be normal/high initially but total body depleted)
Leukocytosis with neutrophilia may be present from stress; evaluate for infectious trigger
Clinical features consistent with DKA (polyuria/polydipsia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul respirations, altered mentation) and/or known diabetes or new-onset diabetes
Exclusion/assessment of alternative causes of high anion gap acidosis (toxic ingestion, lactic acidosis, renal failure) based on history/exam and additional testing

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often elevated (stress leukocytosis); may be higher if infection trigger present
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal or elevated due to hemoconcentration from dehydration
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may be mildly elevated with stress/inflammation
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often low (dilutional hyponatremia); may be normal/high depending on free water deficit
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often normal to high initially despite total body potassium depletion; may fall rapidly with insulin therapy
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Variable; may be normal or elevated (especially as ketosis resolves or with saline resuscitation)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (metabolic acidosis), commonly <18 mEq/L and can be markedly reduced

medications: Regular insulin 0.1 units/kg IV bolus then 0.1 units/kg/hour continuous infusion (or 0.14 units/kg/hour without bolus) with glucose-guided titration
Potassium chloride 20-30 mEq per liter of IV fluids when serum K is 3.3-5.2 mEq/L to maintain K 4.0-5.0 mEq/L (hold if K >5.2; if K <3.3, give 20-30 mEq/hour and delay insulin until K >3.3)
Dextrose (e.g., D5 in 0.45% saline) added to IV fluids when blood glucose approaches 200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) to allow continued insulin until anion gap closes
0.9% sodium chloride IV fluid resuscitation (e.g., 15-20 mL/kg in first hour), then adjust to 0.45% or 0.9% based on corrected sodium and hemodynamics
Sodium bicarbonate IV only if severe acidemia (pH <6.9) with close potassium monitoring (requires additional labs/ABG to justify)
non_pharmacological: Immediate assessment of airway/breathing/circulation, mental status, and volume status; continuous cardiac monitoring
Frequent bedside glucose monitoring (typically hourly) and serial electrolytes/anion gap monitoring (every 2-4 hours) until resolution
Identify and treat precipitating cause (e.g., infection, missed insulin, myocardial ischemia); obtain cultures/imaging/ECG as clinically indicated
Transition to subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin when anion gap closed, patient eating, and clinically stable; overlap IV insulin with first basal dose by 1-2 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess hourly during acute management; repeat electrolytes/CO2 every 2-4 hours until anion gap closes and patient transitions off IV insulin; outpatient diabetes follow-up within 1 week after discharge (earlier if new diagnosis).

must_include: Body is in an acidotic state due to lack of effective insulin leading to ketone production (DKA)
This is a medical emergency requiring IV fluids, insulin, and close monitoring
Discuss likely triggers (infection, missed insulin, new-onset diabetes) and need to search for a cause
IV fluids to correct dehydration and improve circulation
Insulin infusion to stop ketone production and lower glucose safely
Potassium and electrolytes require frequent monitoring and replacement; insulin can drop potassium quickly
Criteria for improvement: closing of anion gap/normalizing bicarbonate and clinical recovery; plan for transition to injections
Potential complications: low blood sugar, low potassium/arrhythmias, cerebral edema (rare, more in children), need for ICU-level monitoring if severe
Clarify penicillin allergy relevance if antibiotics are later needed; discuss alternatives if infection suspected
Invite questions and confirm understanding of warning signs requiring urgent care
Sick-day rules: never stop basal insulin, check glucose/ketones more often, maintain hydration, seek help early
Medication adherence and follow-up for diabetes education
When to seek emergency care: persistent vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, confusion, very high glucose, or positive ketones

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, timeline, diabetes history (known/new), insulin/med adherence, sick contacts/infection symptoms, vomiting/abdominal pain, polyuria/polydipsia, mental status, pregnancy possibility, substance use, and red flags suggesting DKA severity.

Order diagnostic and severity labs for suspected DKA and its triggers: POC glucose, BMP (Na/K/Cl/CO2/BUN/Cr), venous/arterial blood gas, serum beta-hydroxybutyrate (or ketones), CBC, magnesium/phosphate, anion gap calculation, serum osmolality if available, urinalysis with ketones, lactate if concern for sepsis, blood cultures if febrile, pregnancy test if applicable, ECG, and infection workup as indicated (e.g., CXR).

Interpret results to confirm DKA (hyperglycemia, high anion gap metabolic acidosis, ketonemia/ketonuria) and stratify severity, including potassium status and renal function to guide safe insulin and electrolyte replacement.
Make and communicate working diagnosis (DKA) and differential/trigger assessment (infection, missed insulin, new-onset diabetes, MI, pancreatitis, medications).

Critical safety check prior to any prescribing (e.g., insulin, fluids additives, antiemetics, antibiotics if needed). Clarify penicillin allergy details because antibiotics may be required if infection is suspected.

Critical safety check before ordering certain therapies/monitoring plans: ensure contraindication considerations are addressed (e.g., avoid insulin infusion if K+ is dangerously low until repleted; assess fluid contraindications/risks such as heart failure/ESRD; consider pregnancy-related management; assess cerebral edema risk in pediatrics).

Initiate guideline-concordant DKA management: isotonic IV fluids, insulin infusion after confirming K+ is safe, potassium/electrolyte replacement per protocol, dextrose addition when glucose falls, antiemetics/analgesia as appropriate, and ICU-level monitoring if severe.

Non-critical safety check for potential drug interactions if additional meds are prescribed (e.g., QT-prolonging antiemetics with other agents; antibiotics interactions; insulin with beta-blockers masking hypoglycemia).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (DKA emergency), explain treatment and monitoring, address concerns/complications and allergy relevance, and provide sick-day rules and emergency warning signs.
Arrange diabetes education, endocrinology/primary care follow-up, and transition plan after anion gap closure (basal-bolus regimen, supplies, ketone testing, and trigger management).
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, plan documented, and patient understanding confirmed.

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated during active inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Normal to mildly elevated; neutropenia is not expected from JIA itself
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis) during active inflammation
expected_lab_results.Rheumatoid factor (RF): Often negative in early-onset/monogenic JIA; if positive, supports RF-positive polyarticular JIA but is not required

general_concerns: Believes that if symptoms come and go, it probably isn’t serious and doesn’t need evaluation.
Thinks antibiotics are a reasonable first step for most common illnesses and may ask for them “just in case.”
Assumes that normal vital signs or a normal basic blood test would rule out most important problems.
Believes stress is the cause of many physical symptoms and that addressing stress alone will fix them without medical workup.
economic_concerns: Worries that recommended tests might be unnecessary and prefers to start with the cheapest option first.
Assumes generic medications are less effective than brand-name medications.
Concerned about taking time off work for follow-up visits and prefers a single-visit solution if possible.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been having this burning when I pee and I feel like I have to go all the time.” Volunteer: burning with urination, frequent urination, urgency with small amounts, and lower belly pressure/discomfort. If asked, add: cloudy/strong-smelling urine, one or two episodes of slight pink urine, symptoms began 2 days ago and are worsening, no fever/chills, and no flank/side/back pain. Do not volunteer unless asked: you’ve been holding your urine at work, drinking little water lately, and recently used a new scented soap/bubble bath. Be hesitant/embarrassed about sex: only disclose if asked directly; say you had sex a couple days before symptoms and didn’t pee after, and you’re worried about an STI. Mention a misconception: you wondered about a yeast infection due to mild irritation and started cranberry pills yesterday. Express cost/time concerns: prefer the cheapest first, think generics may be weaker, and want a single-visit plan. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

confounders: Recent self-treatment with leftover antibiotics (took 2 doses of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 3 days ago, then stopped due to nausea)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Viral upper respiratory infection (common cold)
required_evidence: Self-limited upper respiratory symptoms (e.g., nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, sore throat, cough) without red flags
No features suggesting bacterial infection requiring antibiotics (e.g., no severe/prolonged fever, no focal lung findings, no exudative tonsillitis with high Centor score)
Stable vital signs and no respiratory distress (if provided/assessed)
Basic labs (if obtained) not showing marked leukocytosis suggestive of bacterial infection

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal or mildly elevated; no marked leukocytosis
expected_lab_results.BUN: Within normal limits (may be mildly elevated if dehydrated)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 1200 mg/day OTC; avoid if contraindicated)
Dextromethorphan 10-20 mg PO every 4 hours as needed for cough (or 30 mg every 6-8 hours as needed)
Guaifenesin 600 mg PO every 12 hours as needed for mucus (with adequate fluids)
Fluticasone nasal spray 50 mcg per spray: 2 sprays per nostril once daily for congestion/post-nasal drip (optional)
non_pharmacological: Rest and oral hydration
Saline nasal spray/irrigation as needed
Warm salt-water gargles and honey (if age-appropriate) for throat/cough
Humidifier/steam inhalation for symptomatic relief
Return precautions for worsening symptoms, shortness of breath, chest pain, persistent high fever, dehydration, or symptoms lasting >10 days
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Self-care with follow-up/reevaluation in 48-72 hours if worsening, or if not improving by 7-10 days; sooner for any red-flag symptoms

must_include: Symptoms are most consistent with a viral upper respiratory infection
Antibiotics are not helpful for viral infections and can cause harm (side effects/resistance)
Expected course: gradual improvement over several days; cough can linger
Focus on symptom relief (fever/pain control, hydration, nasal care)
How to take OTC meds safely (max daily acetaminophen dose; avoid duplicating acetaminophen-containing products)
When to stop and seek care if adverse effects occur
Review penicillin allergy and reassure that no penicillin-class antibiotic is indicated
Discuss red-flag symptoms that require urgent evaluation
Offer shared decision-making about optional symptomatic therapies
Hydration, rest, and hand hygiene to reduce spread
Avoid smoking/vaping and irritants
Masking/cough etiquette if around others, especially high-risk contacts

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key URI history and screen for red flags, duration, severity, exposures, comorbidities, pregnancy, and patient expectations.
Confirm reported penicillin allergy details (reaction type/timing/severity) and document before any prescribing decisions.
Synthesize presentation as most consistent with viral upper respiratory infection; avoid unnecessary testing if uncomplicated.

Explain diagnosis, why antibiotics are not indicated, expected course, symptom-relief plan, safe OTC use including acetaminophen limits, lifestyle/spread reduction, and red-flag return precautions; address concerns and shared decision-making.
Set appropriate follow-up timeframe and contingency plan (worsening, persistence beyond expected course, or red flags).
Close encounter after ensuring understanding and opportunity for questions.

nl_assertions: Agent gathers a focused URI history: onset/duration, fever, cough character, sore throat, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, myalgias, dyspnea/wheeze, chest pain, ear pain, sinus pain/pressure, sick contacts, COVID/flu exposure/testing status, and current medications/OTC use.

Agent actively screens for red flags requiring urgent evaluation: trouble breathing, hypoxia signs, chest pain, confusion, severe dehydration, persistent high fever, neck stiffness, severe unilateral facial pain/swelling, immunocompromised status, or symptoms rapidly worsening.

Agent explains that symptoms are most consistent with a viral upper respiratory infection and clearly states antibiotics are not helpful for viral infections and can cause harm (side effects and antimicrobial resistance).
Agent sets expectations for the clinical course: gradual improvement over several days; cough may linger longer than other symptoms.
Agent provides a symptom-focused treatment plan: hydration, rest, fever/pain control, nasal saline/steam, and other appropriate OTC options with shared decision-making.

Agent provides OTC safety counseling including maximum daily acetaminophen dose (do not exceed 3,000–4,000 mg/day depending on patient factors) and explicitly warns to avoid duplicating acetaminophen-containing combination products.
Agent instructs when to stop OTC medications and seek care if adverse effects occur (e.g., rash, breathing difficulty, GI bleeding symptoms, severe dizziness, allergic reaction).
Agent reviews the penicillin allergy and reassures that no penicillin-class antibiotic is indicated for uncomplicated viral URI.
Agent includes lifestyle and infection-control education: hydration, rest, hand hygiene, cough etiquette/masking around others—especially high-risk contacts—and avoiding smoking/vaping/irritants.
Agent avoids ordering unnecessary labs/imaging for uncomplicated viral URI at L0 difficulty and avoids prescribing antibiotics.

If the agent chooses to prescribe any medication, it must perform contraindication and (noncritical) interaction checks before prescribing; failure to do allergy_check or contraindication_check before prescribing is a critical error.
Agent uses clear, non-alarmist language, checks understanding, and offers follow-up/return precautions.

minimal_paths.description: Uncomplicated viral URI managed with education and symptomatic care; no diagnostic testing or antibiotics.
steps: ASK: focused URI history and red-flag screen

EDUCATE: diagnosis + no antibiotics + expected course + symptom relief + OTC safety (max acetaminophen, avoid duplication) + adverse effects/stop rules + red flags + lifestyle/spread reduction + shared decision-making

acceptable_variants.description: Agent discusses optional home COVID/flu testing as shared decision-making but does not require ordering labs; still treats as viral URI if uncomplicated and stable.
steps: EDUCATE (includes optional testing discussion and isolation/masking guidance as appropriate)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent recommends a specific OTC regimen (e.g., acetaminophen or ibuprofen, saline spray, honey for cough if age-appropriate) without prescribing; includes contraindication screening in history rather than tool checks since no prescription is made.
